name: Sierra de Miravete Loop
name_original: Puerto de Miravete a Higuera de Albalat — circular por el Campillo de Deleitosa
description: This 28.9-mile (46.5 km) moderate road cycling route from Puerto de Miravete to Higuera de Albalat offers challenging climbs and scenic Extr
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Deleitosa traverse a landscape characterized by mountains, valleys, and extensive dehesa ecosystems. The region is situated within the Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve and the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara Geopark, offering diverse and protected natural environments. Cyclists encounter varied topography, from challenging climbs in the Sierra de la Breña to picturesque routes through wooded pastures towards the Almonte River. This area provides a mix of terrain suitable for different road cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Deleitosa The most popular road cycling route is Sierra de Miravete Loop, a 28.9 miles (46.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 38 minutes to complete. This route offers varied topography through the Sierra de Miravete. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Deleitosa from Puerto de Miravete — circular by Higuera de Albalat, a difficult 48.2 miles (77.6 km) path. It features significant elevation changes within the Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve. Local road cyclists also love the Higuera de Albalat to Campillo de Deleitosa — circular via the Puerto de Campillo, a 22.1 miles (35.6 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and dehesa ecosystems, often completed in about 2 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Deleitosa is defined by mountains and valleys, dehesa ecosystems, and protected natural areas. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate routes to more challenging climbs. The routes in Deleitosa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Deleitosa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Deleitosa?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Deleitosa. These routes offer a variety of experiences, from moderate rides to more challenging climbs through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Deleitosa?
A: Road cycling routes in Deleitosa traverse a varied landscape, including mountainous areas like the Sierra de la Breña, valleys, and extensive dehesa ecosystems. You'll find both challenging climbs and picturesque routes through wooded pastures towards the Almonte River, all within the protected Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve and Villuercas-Ibores-Jara Geopark.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Deleitosa?
A: Yes, Deleitosa offers several challenging routes for experienced road cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371616402/deleitosa-from-puerto-de-miravete-circular-by-higuera-de-albalat" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Deleitosa from Puerto de Miravete — circular by Higuera de Albalat&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 77.6 km path with significant elevation changes within the Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40303984" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Summit – Guadalupe Viaduct loop from Cabañas del Castillo&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 85 km with over 2000 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for intermediate cyclists?
A: Absolutely. Intermediate cyclists can enjoy routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371389078/sierra-de-miravete-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Miravete Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 46.5 km trail that takes about 2 hours 38 minutes to complete, offering varied topography. Another moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371591359/higuera-de-albalat-to-campillo-de-deleitosa-circular-via-the-puerto-de-campillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Higuera de Albalat to Campillo de Deleitosa — circular via the Puerto de Campillo&lt;/a&gt;, a 35.6 km route through mountainous terrain and dehesa ecosystems.

Q: What natural features or landscapes will I see while road cycling in Deleitosa?
A: While road cycling around Deleitosa, you'll experience a rich diversity of natural features. The region is part of the Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve and the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara Geopark, offering stunning views of mountains, valleys, and extensive dehesa (wooded pastures) with holm and cork oaks. You might also encounter the Almonte River and various gorges, contributing to the scenic beauty and geological interest.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, several historical landmarks and attractions can be incorporated into your cycling routes. You could cycle towards the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5579771" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabañas del Castillo Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which offers spectacular views. Another point of interest is the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5477194" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acueducto de las Herrerías&lt;/a&gt; near Campillo de Deleitosa, known for its historical significance and beautiful natural surroundings.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in the Deleitosa area?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Deleitosa are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371389078/sierra-de-miravete-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Miravete Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371616402/deleitosa-from-puerto-de-miravete-circular-by-higuera-de-albalat" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Deleitosa from Puerto de Miravete — circular by Higuera de Albalat&lt;/a&gt;, providing convenient options for exploring the region.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Deleitosa?
A: The best time for road cycling in Deleitosa, located in Extremadura, is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter days of winter. This allows for more comfortable and enjoyable rides through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Deleitosa?
A: The road cycling routes around Deleitosa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Deleitosa's varied terrain, often praising the diverse topography and the stunning natural environments within the protected areas.

Q: Are there options for parking when I go road cycling in Deleitosa?
A: While specific parking areas for cyclists are not detailed, Deleitosa is a small town, and you can typically find parking in or around the town center. It's advisable to look for public parking spaces near the start of your chosen route or inquire locally upon arrival for the most convenient options.

Q: Are there any very long road cycling routes for endurance training in Deleitosa?
A: Yes, for endurance training, you can find longer routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38172425" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Summit – Castañar de Ibor Town Square loop from Deleitosa&lt;/a&gt;. This route spans over 95 km with significant elevation gain, offering a substantial challenge for long-distance riders.
tips: Cycle to the Miravete mountain pass. From this point you can see the ruins of the Zúñigas fortification from the 16th century. Today, eclipsed by communication antennas.
At the foot of the Sierra de la Caldilla, is the small historic town of Higuera. Stop by the charming San Sebastián church if you need to rest and grab a bite to eat.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a 'hard road ride' and requires very good fitness due to its significant elevation gain of over 1,300 meters. While the surface is predominantly well-paved, cyclists should be prepared for sustained climbs, including a tough section from Valdecañas to Higuera via the Descuernacabras gorge, and the long, gradual ascent of Puerto de Miravete. It's best suited for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on well-paved roads and asphalt, making it suitable for racebikes. You'll encounter three main climbs: a short one to Campillo (720 m), a challenging ascent from Valdecañas to Higuera through the Descuernacabras gorge, and the long, gradual climb of the Puerto de Miravete mountain pass (666 m). While some sections are relatively flat, expect demanding climbs.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle through the beautiful Extremaduran landscape, characterized by forests of chestnut, oak, and holm oak trees. The route is situated within the Monfragüe National Park and Biosphere Reserve, offering picturesque views of dehesa (Mediterranean pastures) and possibly reservoirs. Expect a strong connection to untamed nature and diverse wildlife, especially large birds.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, a key point is the Miravete Mountain Pass, where you can observe the ruins of the 16th-century Zúñigas fortification. You'll also pass by the charming Church of San Sebastián of Higuera de Albalat. The natural beauty of the Monfragüe area, with its cork and holm oaks, provides stunning vistas throughout the ride.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking facilities aren't detailed, you can typically find parking near the starting points in towns like Puerto de Miravete or Campillo de Deleitosa. Look for available street parking or designated areas within these villages to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region of Extremadura can get very hot in summer. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, with milder weather and beautiful natural colors. Winter can also be suitable, though temperatures will be cooler.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally allowed in natural areas, bringing a dog on a challenging road bike ride is not typically recommended for their safety or comfort. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer designed for pets and consider the strenuous nature of the route.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a specific permit or to pay an entrance fee to cycle this public road route. While the trail passes through the Monfragüe National Park and Biosphere Reserve, access to the roads within the park is generally free for cyclists. Always respect local regulations and wildlife.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several official trails. You'll cross sections of the Garganta de los Nogales, Camino Natural del Tajo, La Breña, La Piñuela, Cordel de Torrecillas de la Tiesa, Calleja de Fuente, and various segments of the Cañada Real del Puerto del Pico and Cañada Real del Puerto del Pico y Miravete.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through small villages like Higuera de Albalat and Campillo de Deleitosa. These towns may offer limited options for cafes or small shops where you can refuel or purchase water. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially given the challenging nature of the ride and potential distances between services.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, pack essentials like plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire for the weather, sun protection, and a first-aid kit. A fully charged phone and a GPS device with the route loaded are also highly recommended.
